title: Postprandial burning chest discomfort in a polypharmacy patient with cardiac risk factors

chart.oneLiner: 58F with type 2 diabetes, hypertension on a thiazide, diabetic peripheral neuropathy on amitriptyline, and chronic GERD presents with several weeks of intermittent burning retrosternal discomfort, worse after large meals and when lying flat at night, partly relieved by antacids; she is anxious because a relative recently had a heart attack.

opening: I keep getting this burning in my chest, mostly after I eat or when I lie down at night. Tums help a little. My brother just had a heart attack, so honestly I'm scared this is my heart.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 58-year-old woman, comfortable at rest, anxious about her heart because her brother recently had an MI. Cooperative and conversational.

HPI:
- Intermittent BURNING retrosternal discomfort for several weeks, gradually more frequent.
- Clearly worse AFTER large or fatty meals and when LYING FLAT at night; partly relieved by sitting up and by antacids.
- Associated sour/acid taste in the mouth and occasional regurgitation, worse at night (reveal only if asked about associated symptoms or reflux).
- NOT reliably exertional — it comes on at rest and after meals, not specifically with walking or climbing stairs; she can walk her usual errands without bringing it on (reveal the exertional question only when asked).
- NO radiation to the arm or jaw, NO sweating, NO shortness of breath with the pain, NO pleuritic or positional-with-breathing component, NO tearing/ripping quality (these are negative — only if asked).
- Alarm features: she has felt a bit more TIRED lately and wonders if her stools have looked darker once or twice but is not sure; NO frank black tarry stools she is certain about, NO vomiting blood, NO trouble swallowing, NO unintentional weight loss (reveal only if asked about swallowing, weight, fatigue, or GI bleeding).

OLDCARTS: intermittent over several weeks; retrosternal; episodes last up to an hour; burning quality; worse postprandial and supine, partly relieved by antacids; severity 4-5/10.

PMH: type 2 diabetes (about 8 years); hypertension; diabetic peripheral neuropathy of the feet; chronic GERD/reflux for years; mild knee osteoarthritis.
MEDICATIONS (polypharmacy — reveal the full list when asked about medications): metformin; lisinopril; HYDROCHLOROTHIAZIDE (a 'water pill' for blood pressure); AMITRIPTYLINE at night for the burning foot pain from neuropathy; CITALOPRAM for low mood; ONDANSETRON occasionally for nausea; over-the-counter antacids and famotidine as needed; occasional ibuprofen for knee pain. ALLERGIES: none.
FAMILY: brother had a myocardial infarction at age 61 (about a month ago) — this is why she is frightened. Father had diabetes.
SOCIAL: never smoked; one or two glasses of wine on weekends (worsens the burning); drinks coffee daily; works at a desk; walks for errands without chest pain.
ROS: mild fatigue (see alarm features); otherwise the cardiac/respiratory negatives above; no palpitations, no syncope, no dizziness.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: well-appearing, comfortable, anxious but in no acute distress.
- Vitals: BP 138/84, HR 72, RR 15, 36.8C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmur, rub, or gallop; no radiation.
- Lungs: clear bilaterally; no crackles.
- Chest wall: no reproducible tenderness to palpation.
- Abdomen: soft, mild epigastric tenderness, no guarding or rebound.
- Conjunctiva/mucous membranes: slightly pale conjunctivae (consistent with mild anemia) if specifically inspected.
- Extremities: no edema, distal pulses symmetric; reduced light-touch/pinprick sensation in a stocking distribution (diabetic neuropathy) if sensation is tested.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the regurgitation/sour taste, the exertional vs non-exertional detail, the fatigue/dark-stool alarm features, the family history of MI specifics, or the full medication list unless asked the relevant question (associated symptoms, reflux, exertion, swallowing, weight, GI bleeding/stool color, family history, or medications).
- Reveal exam findings (pale conjunctivae, neuropathy, epigastric tenderness) only when that specific exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Mild MICROCYTIC anemia: Hgb 10.6 g/dL (baseline ~12), MCV 76 fL, Plt 295, WBC 7.1. Pattern suggests chronic iron-deficiency/occult GI blood loss rather than acute hemorrhage.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 3.1 (LOW), Cl 102, HCO3 26, BUN 16, Cr 0.9 (normal renal function), Glucose 162. Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 138 - (102 + 26) = 138 - 128 = 10 (NORMAL, no anion-gap acidosis). The hypokalemia is consistent with her thiazide.
labs.Magnesium: Mg 1.3 mg/dL (LOW). Combined with the hypokalemia (thiazide + PPI-associated losses), this is the electrolyte substrate for QT prolongation seen on the EKG.
labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin NEGATIVE on serial testing (0 h and 3 h, both below the assay's upper reference limit with no delta) — argues strongly against acute coronary syndrome.
labs.HbA1c: 8.4% — confirms longstanding, suboptimally controlled type 2 diabetes (a cardiac risk factor that keeps ACS on the differential until excluded).
labs.FOBT: Fecal occult blood test POSITIVE — corroborates occult GI blood loss as the source of the iron-deficiency anemia; mandates outpatient endoscopic evaluation.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical in this 58-year-old woman with diabetes, hypertension, and a frightening family history, list your differential diagnosis and note your leading diagnosis. Name THREE mechanisms or etiologies by which gastroesophageal reflux produces burning chest pain, and state explicitly which must-not-miss cardiac diagnosis you will exclude before committing.
steps.rubric: GERD / esophageal reflux as the leading diagnosis given the postprandial/supine burning, sour taste, antacid response, and non-exertional pattern (leading,5). Acute coronary syndrome / stable angina — the genuine competing diagnosis that MUST be excluded given diabetes, HTN, age, and a brother with a recent MI (CANT-MISS,6). Names THREE GERD mechanisms — e.g. lower-esophageal-sphincter incompetence/transient relaxations, acid-mucosal irritation of esophageal chemoreceptors, esophageal acid-provoked dysmotility/spasm, delayed gastric emptying (diabetic gastroparesis), or a hiatal hernia reducing the anti-reflux barrier (core, up to 3, 1 each). Esophageal spasm (core,2). Peptic ulcer disease — supported by NSAID use and the anemia (core,2). Pericarditis (bonus,2). Musculoskeletal/costochondral (bonus,1). States the diabetic/family-history risk profile keeps ACS on the table until troponin/EKG return (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on GERD without excluding cardiac causes in a diabetic with cardiac risk factors (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: GERD — several weeks of postprandial and supine retrosternal burning with a sour taste, partial antacid response, and a non-exertional pattern. The genuine competitor that must be excluded first is cardiac ischemia (acute coronary syndrome / stable angina): she has diabetes (often atypical/silent presentations), hypertension, and a brother with a recent MI, so a normal EKG and serial troponin are required before anchoring on reflux. Three mechanisms by which GERD causes burning chest pain: (1) lower-esophageal-sphincter incompetence / transient LES relaxations allowing acid reflux; (2) direct acid irritation of esophageal mucosal chemoreceptors; (3) acid-provoked esophageal dysmotility/spasm (and delayed gastric emptying from diabetic gastroparesis, or a hiatal hernia weakening the anti-reflux barrier). Also reasonable: peptic ulcer disease (NSAID use, anemia), esophageal spasm, pericarditis, musculoskeletal pain.

criticalActions.item: Acute coronary syndrome / stable angina excluded before committing (cardiac must-not-miss)

coreActions.item: GERD / esophageal reflux as leading diagnosis
Names three mechanisms/etiologies of GERD-related chest pain
States diabetic/family-history risk profile keeps ACS on the table until troponin/EKG return

penalties.item: Anchoring on GERD without excluding cardiac causes in a diabetic with risk factors

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order, and why?
steps.rubric: 12-lead EKG to exclude an acute cardiac cause (CANT-MISS,5). Serial high-sensitivity troponin given the cardiac risk profile (core,3). CBC to evaluate the suspected anemia / occult GI bleed, with iron studies and a stool occult blood test (core,3). BMP plus a MAGNESIUM level — important because she is on a thiazide and the EKG shows QT prolongation (core,2). HbA1c / glucose to characterize her diabetes risk (core,1). CXR (bonus,1). Empiric PPI trial as diagnostic-and-therapeutic once cardiac causes are addressed (bonus,1). Outpatient upper endoscopy given anemia + positive occult blood (bonus,1). PENALTY: failing to obtain an EKG/troponin in a diabetic with cardiac risk factors (-3); ignoring the anemia (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: 12-lead EKG and serial high-sensitivity troponin to exclude ACS (mandatory given diabetes, HTN, and the family history). CBC with iron studies and a stool occult blood test to work up the suspected anemia/occult GI bleed. BMP AND a magnesium level (she is on a thiazide; electrolyte derangements matter for the QT). HbA1c. CXR. Once cardiac causes are addressed, an empiric PPI trial is both diagnostic and therapeutic, and outpatient upper endoscopy is warranted given anemia plus a positive occult blood test.

criticalActions.item: 12-lead EKG to exclude acute cardiac cause

coreActions.item: CBC with iron studies / stool occult blood for the anemia
BMP with a magnesium level (thiazide, QT)

bonusActions.item: Outpatient upper endoscopy given anemia + positive occult blood

penalties.item: Failing to obtain EKG/troponin in a diabetic with cardiac risk factors

requiredTests: 12-lead EKG to exclude acute cardiac cause
optionalTests: CBC with iron studies / stool occult blood for the anemia
BMP with a magnesium level (thiazide, QT)
orderSensitiveActions: 12-lead EKG to exclude acute cardiac cause

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's own 12-lead EKG. Interpret it systematically, then state how it fits the clinical picture and what it means for her medications.
steps.rubric: The key abnormality is a PROLONGED QT interval (prolonged QTc) with sinus rhythm and NO acute ischemic ST/T changes. CREDIT: recognizes the prolonged QTc (critical); attributes it to her QT-prolonging polypharmacy (amitriptyline, citalopram, ondansetron) PLUS hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia (core); states there is no acute ischemia to suggest ACS, which supports the GERD pathway (core); systematic read of rate/rhythm/axis/intervals (core). PENALTY: calling the study normal or over-reading it as a STEMI/ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: attributes QT prolongation to QT-prolonging drugs plus low K and low Mg
criticalFindings: Prolonged QT interval (drug- and electrolyte-related) without acute ischemia.
commonMistakes: calling the study normal and missing the long QT
over-reading benign findings as a STEMI/ischemia
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm with a PROLONGED QTc and no acute ischemic ST/T changes. The long QT is iatrogenic/metabolic: she is on multiple QT-prolonging agents (amitriptyline, citalopram, ondansetron) and is hypokalemic and hypomagnesemic. The absence of acute ischemia supports the reflux explanation for her pain rather than ACS, but the prolonged QT is itself a safety problem that must be corrected (replete K and Mg, reconcile/stop QT-prolonging drugs) before discharge.

criticalActions: Prolonged QT interval (drug- and electrolyte-related) without acute ischemia.
coreActions: attributes QT prolongation to QT-prolonging drugs plus low K and low Mg

penalties.label: calls the study normal / over-reads as STEMI / misses the long QT

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = hiatal hernia: a retrocardiac soft-tissue density with an air-fluid level behind the heart; a benign finding that supports the reflux mechanism but is not the cause of an acute event. CREDIT: identifies the retrocardiac air-fluid level (6); recognizes the hiatal hernia as benign and as anatomic support for reflux, not an acute process (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / mistaking the retrocardiac gas shadow for a cavitary lesion or abscess (-3).
keyFindings: identifies a hiatal hernia as benign and supportive of reflux
commonMistakes: mistaking the retrocardiac gas shadow for a cavitary lesion or abscess
steps.idealAnswer: Hiatal hernia: retrocardiac soft-tissue density with an air-fluid level behind the heart. Lungs are clear and the cardiomediastinal silhouette is normal. The hernia weakens the anti-reflux barrier and supports the GERD mechanism, but it is a benign incidental finding, not the cause of an acute event.

coreActions: identifies a hiatal hernia as benign and supportive of reflux

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The serial troponins are negative, the EKG shows a prolonged QT without ischemia, and labs show mild microcytic anemia with a positive occult blood test plus hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. What is your leading diagnosis now, what single test best discriminates the residual ambiguity, and what concurrent problems must you also address?
steps.rubric: Commits to GERD as the most likely cause of the chest pain, justified by the postprandial/supine burning, antacid response, non-exertional pattern, and a reassuring cardiac workup (negative serial troponin, no ischemia on EKG). Names the discriminating test for residual ambiguity — outpatient stress testing/coronary evaluation if any atypical or exertional features persist, and upper endoscopy as the definitive test for the esophageal/peptic source given the anemia and positive occult blood. Recognizes the multi-system concurrent problems as SEPARATE issues: iron-deficiency anemia from occult GI bleeding (needs endoscopy), a drug- and electrolyte-induced prolonged QT (needs K/Mg repletion and medication reconciliation), and suboptimally controlled diabetes. Acknowledges residual diagnostic uncertainty appropriately.
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: GERD. The pain is postprandial and supine, partly antacid-responsive, non-exertional, and the cardiac workup is reassuring (negative serial high-sensitivity troponin and no acute ischemia on EKG), which makes acute coronary syndrome very unlikely. Residual ambiguity: because she is diabetic with risk factors, if any exertional or atypical features persist, outpatient stress testing / coronary evaluation is the best discriminator for stable angina; for the esophageal/peptic source, upper endoscopy is the definitive discriminator and is independently indicated by the iron-deficiency anemia with a positive occult blood test. Three concurrent problems to address separately: (1) iron-deficiency anemia from occult GI blood loss (endoscopy, iron studies, NSAID cessation); (2) a prolonged QT from QT-prolonging polypharmacy plus hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia (replete K and Mg, reconcile/stop offending drugs); and (3) poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%).

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition? State explicitly whether this patient can be safely discharged and what must be in place first.
steps.rubric: Per MGH GERD: start a PPI trial, e.g. omeprazole 20 mg daily for 8 weeks (CANT-MISS,5); lifestyle modification - weight loss, trigger avoidance, no meals before bed, head-of-bed elevation, reduce alcohol/coffee (4). MEDICATION RECONCILIATION addressing the prolonged QT - stop/replace QT-prolonging agents (amitriptyline, citalopram, ondansetron) and stop the NSAID that contributes to GI bleeding (CANT-MISS,4); REPLETE potassium and magnesium and correct the QT before discharge (3); arrange outpatient upper endoscopy / GI referral for the anemia + positive occult blood, and start iron (3). SAFE-DISCHARGE DECISION: she can be discharged home AFTER serial troponins are negative, the EKG/QT is reviewed and electrolytes repleted, with med reconciliation done, return precautions given, and close PCP/cardiology and GI follow-up arranged (CANT-MISS,4); reassure that the cardiac workup is reassuring while explaining the residual outpatient cardiac evaluation if symptoms persist (2). PENALTY: discharging without addressing the prolonged QT / occult GI bleed (-3); adding another QT-prolonging drug (-2); invasive cardiac workup with no indication after a negative workup (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: GERD treatment: start a PPI (omeprazole 20 mg before breakfast) for an 8-week trial; lifestyle modification - weight loss, avoid trigger foods and late meals, elevate the head of the bed, reduce alcohol and coffee. Medication reconciliation is central here: stop or replace the QT-prolonging agents (amitriptyline, citalopram, ondansetron) given the prolonged QT, and stop the ibuprofen, which contributes to her occult GI bleeding; choose a neuropathy agent that does not prolong the QT. Replete potassium and magnesium and confirm the QT has improved before discharge. Arrange outpatient GI referral for upper endoscopy given the iron-deficiency anemia and positive occult blood test, and start oral iron. SAFE-DISCHARGE DECISION: she CAN go home rather than be admitted, but only after serial troponins are negative, the EKG/QT has been reviewed and electrolytes repleted, medications reconciled, strict return precautions given (worsening or exertional chest pain, syncope, palpitations, melena), and close follow-up arranged with her PCP/cardiology (for residual cardiac risk-stratification if symptoms persist) and GI. Reassure her about the reassuring cardiac workup while being honest about the planned outpatient evaluation.

criticalActions.item: Medication reconciliation: stop/replace QT-prolonging drugs and the NSAID
Safe-discharge decision: home only after negative troponins, QT/electrolytes corrected, med rec, return precautions, and close PCP/cardiology + GI follow-up

coreActions.item: Replete potassium and magnesium and correct the QT before discharge
Outpatient endoscopy / GI referral and iron for the anemia + occult blood
Reassure about the cardiac workup while explaining residual outpatient cardiac evaluation

penalties.item: Discharging without addressing the prolonged QT / occult GI bleed

requiredActions: Medication reconciliation: stop/replace QT-prolonging drugs and the NSAID
Safe-discharge decision: home only after negative troponins, QT/electrolytes corrected, med rec, return precautions, and close PCP/cardiology + GI follow-up
optionalActions: Replete potassium and magnesium and correct the QT before discharge
Outpatient endoscopy / GI referral and iron for the anemia + occult blood
Reassure about the cardiac workup while explaining residual outpatient cardiac evaluation
unsafeActions: Discharging without addressing the prolonged QT / occult GI bleed
steps.disposition: Discharge home after med reconciliation, electrolyte/QT correction, and arranged close follow-up

ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm with a prolonged QTc and no acute ischemic ST/T changes; drug- (anticholinergic/QT-prolonging) and electrolyte-related (low K, low Mg) long QT.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus rhythm with prolonged QTc, no acute ischemia. Self-host with appropriate credit.

ekg.clinicalVignette: A 58-year-old woman on amitriptyline, citalopram, and ondansetron, hypokalemic and hypomagnesemic, has her chest pain screened for a cardiac cause before discharge.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Retrocardiac soft-tissue opacity with an air-fluid level behind the heart (hiatal hernia); lungs clear.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Retrocardiac soft-tissue opacity with an air-fluid level behind the heart (hiatal hernia). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing hiatal hernia retrocardiac air-fluid level
searchTerms: hiatal hernia chest x-ray air fluid level
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 58-year-old woman with postprandial retrosternal burning and nocturnal regurgitation has a chest film showing a retrocardiac air-fluid level.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmur, rub, or gallop; no radiation.
Abdomen: soft, mild epigastric tenderness, no guarding or rebound.
Chest wall: no reproducible tenderness to palpation (argues against a musculoskeletal cause).
Slightly pale conjunctivae, consistent with mild anemia.
Reduced light-touch and pinprick sensation in a stocking distribution in both feet (diabetic peripheral neuropathy).
Extremities: no peripheral edema; distal pulses symmetric.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 58F with type 2 diabetes, hypertension on a thiazide, diabetic neuropathy on amitriptyline, and chronic reflux presents with several weeks of postprandial and supine retrosternal burning, frightened by a brother's recent MI. The final diagnosis is GERD, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and excluding the genuine competing cardiac diagnosis (negative serial troponin, no ischemia on EKG). The case layers concurrent problems: iron-deficiency anemia from occult GI blood loss (positive occult blood), a drug- and electrolyte-induced prolonged QT (hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia on QT-prolonging polypharmacy), and suboptimally controlled diabetes.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmur, rub, or gallop; no radiation.
Abdomen: soft, mild epigastric tenderness, no guarding or rebound.
Chest wall: no reproducible tenderness to palpation (argues against a musculoskeletal cause).
Slightly pale conjunctivae, consistent with mild anemia.
Reduced light-touch and pinprick sensation in a stocking distribution in both feet (diabetic peripheral neuropathy).
Extremities: no peripheral edema; distal pulses symmetric.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the cardiac risk profile and the patient's fear.
Elicit the focused history, including the exertional relationship, alarm features, family cardiac history, and full medication list only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam to reveal pale conjunctivae, epigastric tenderness, neuropathy, and a reassuring cardiac/chest-wall exam.
Build a differential led by GERD while explicitly excluding the competing cardiac diagnosis and naming GERD mechanisms.
Order EKG and serial troponin to exclude ACS, plus CBC/iron/occult blood, BMP with magnesium, and HbA1c; interpret the EKG's prolonged QT and the hiatal hernia on CXR.
Commit to GERD after a reassuring cardiac workup, address the concurrent anemia, prolonged QT, and diabetes, and make a safe-discharge disposition with med reconciliation and close follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on GERD without excluding cardiac causes in a diabetic with risk factors
Missing the prolonged QT and the QT-prolonging polypharmacy plus low K/Mg
Ignoring the iron-deficiency anemia and positive occult blood
Discharging without medication reconciliation or electrolyte/QT correction
managementPearls: Medication reconciliation: stop/replace QT-prolonging drugs and the NSAID
Safe discharge only after negative troponins, QT/electrolytes corrected, and close follow-up
criticalWorkupItems: 12-lead EKG to exclude acute cardiac cause
criticalInterpretationItems: Prolonged QT interval (drug- and electrolyte-related) without acute ischemia.
criticalManagementItems: Medication reconciliation: stop/replace QT-prolonging drugs and the NSAID
Safe-discharge decision: home only after negative troponins, QT/electrolytes corrected, med rec, return precautions, and close PCP/cardiology + GI follow-up
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Prolonged QT interval (drug- and electrolyte-related) without acute ischemia.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Prolonged QT interval (drug- and electrolyte-related) without acute ischemia.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental or iatrogenic abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name the prolonged QT, attribute it, and act on it.
Integrate the EKG with the medication list and electrolytes: QT-prolonging drugs plus low K and low Mg are the cause here.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, including the absence of acute ischemia.

items.item: Acknowledges and addresses the patient's fear about her heart
Asks permission before sensitive questions (mood, alcohol)
Explains next steps and the discharge plan in patient-friendly language
